title: Het sollicitatiegesprek
context: You are attending a job interview for a part-time position at a supermarket. The manager asks about your experience and availability.

lines.speaker: Manager
lines.dutch: Goedemorgen, u bent vast mevrouw Yilmaz. Welkom! Komt u verder en gaat u zitten.
lines.english: Good morning, you must be Mrs. Yilmaz. Welcome! Come in and have a seat.
lines.note: 'U bent vast...' is a polite way of saying 'you must be...'.

lines.speaker: Sollicitant
lines.dutch: Goedemorgen. Dank u wel. Ik vind het fijn dat ik mocht langskomen voor een gesprek.
lines.english: Good morning. Thank you. I'm glad I was able to come in for an interview.

lines.speaker: Manager
lines.dutch: Uiteraard. Kunt u zich even kort voorstellen?
lines.english: Of course. Could you briefly introduce yourself?

lines.speaker: Sollicitant
lines.dutch: Natuurlijk. Mijn naam is Elif Yilmaz. Ik ben dertig jaar oud en ik woon sinds twee jaar in Amsterdam. Ik heb in mijn thuisland in een winkel gewerkt.
lines.english: Of course. My name is Elif Yilmaz. I am thirty years old and I have been living in Amsterdam for two years. I worked in a shop in my home country.

lines.speaker: Manager
lines.dutch: Wat voor werkervaring hebt u in Nederland?
lines.english: What work experience do you have in the Netherlands?

lines.speaker: Sollicitant
lines.dutch: Ik heb als vrijwilliger gewerkt bij een buurthuis. Daar hielp ik met het organiseren van activiteiten en het ontvangen van bezoekers.
lines.english: I worked as a volunteer at a community center. There I helped with organizing activities and welcoming visitors.
lines.note: 'Buurthuis' is a community center. Volunteering is valued on Dutch CVs.

lines.speaker: Manager
lines.dutch: Dat klinkt goed. Hoe is uw Nederlands? Want u moet klanten kunnen helpen.
lines.english: That sounds good. How is your Dutch? Because you need to be able to help customers.

lines.speaker: Sollicitant
lines.dutch: Ik volg een inburgeringscursus en mijn Nederlands wordt steeds beter. Ik kan goed met mensen omgaan.
lines.english: I'm taking a civic integration course and my Dutch is getting better all the time. I'm good with people.
lines.note: 'Met mensen omgaan' means 'to deal/interact with people'.

lines.speaker: Manager
lines.dutch: Mooi. De functie is voor vierentwintig uur per week. U zou op maandag, woensdag en vrijdag werken. Past dat?
lines.english: Great. The position is for twenty-four hours per week. You would work on Monday, Wednesday, and Friday. Does that work?

lines.speaker: Sollicitant
lines.dutch: Ja, dat past goed. Ik ben ook beschikbaar in het weekend als dat nodig is.
lines.english: Yes, that works well. I'm also available on weekends if needed.

lines.speaker: Manager
lines.dutch: Wat goed. Het salaris is veertien euro per uur. U krijgt ook vakantiegeld en een reiskostenvergoeding.
lines.english: Wonderful. The salary is fourteen euros per hour. You also receive holiday pay and a travel allowance.
lines.note: 'Vakantiegeld' is holiday pay (8% of annual salary), paid in May. This is unique to the Netherlands.

lines.speaker: Sollicitant
lines.dutch: Dat klinkt goed. Wanneer zou ik kunnen beginnen?
lines.english: That sounds good. When could I start?

lines.speaker: Manager
lines.dutch: We zoeken iemand die volgende maand kan beginnen. Is dat mogelijk?
lines.english: We're looking for someone who can start next month. Is that possible?

lines.speaker: Sollicitant
lines.dutch: Ja, dat is geen probleem. Ik kan volgende maand beginnen.
lines.english: Yes, that's no problem. I can start next month.

lines.speaker: Manager
lines.dutch: Uitstekend. Wij laten u binnen een week iets weten. Heeft u nog vragen?
lines.english: Excellent. We'll let you know within a week. Do you have any questions?
lines.note: 'Iets laten weten' means 'to let someone know'.

keyPhrases.usage: This is a standard opening question in Dutch job interviews.
Use this to talk about your work experience.
Use this to discuss your schedule and availability.
A good question to ask at the end of an interview.
In the Netherlands, it is acceptable to ask about salary during the interview.

culturalNote: The Dutch are known for their directness (directheid) in business and at work. It is perfectly normal to ask about salary, working hours, and holiday days during a job interview. Vakantiegeld (holiday pay) is a legal right in the Netherlands: you receive 8% of your gross annual salary, usually paid in May. Dutch workplaces tend to have a flat hierarchy, and colleagues often address each other with 'je' (informal you) rather than 'u' (formal you), even with managers.
